summary: I worked at the Community Council of the Atlanta area, which was a social and welfare planning agency. It was the place where I was involved with the Information and Referral system before it was moved to the United Appeals office. The council no longer exists, but it was an important part of my early career in community service and welfare planning.

The Governor's Council on Human Relations in Georgia was established by Governor Jimmy Carter in October 1971. I served as the coordinator of this council, which had equal black and white participation and aimed to foster communication and mediate racial issues across the state. Although it lacked enforcement powers and a separate budget, it played a vital role in opening dialogue and supporting biracial councils in Georgia's counties.

I never worked on Jimmy Carter's campaign and didn't know him personally before joining his office. He established the Governor's Council on Human Relations and showed a genuine interest in involving black people in state government. Through my role, I helped connect him with key black community leaders like Andrew Young and Jesse Hill, and I witnessed his commitment to improving race relations in Georgia during the early 1970s.

I worked with Jesse Hill, who was the president of Atlanta Life Insurance Company and chairman of the All Citizens Registration Committee. I volunteered with him during voter registration drives, which was one of my few political involvements before joining the governor's office. Jesse Hill was a key figure in the black community and helped mobilize voter participation in Georgia.
cues: president of Atlanta Life Insurance Company
chairman of All Citizens Registration Committee

summary: Atlanta Life Insurance Company was led by Jesse Hill, with whom I volunteered on voter registration efforts. The company was significant in the black community, and Jesse Hill's leadership there connected me indirectly to important civic activities in Atlanta and Georgia. My work with Jesse Hill at Atlanta Life helped me gain experience in community organizing.

The All Citizens Registration Committee, chaired by Jesse Hill, was involved in voter registration drives in Georgia. I volunteered with this committee, which was part of my limited political activity before working in Governor Carter's office. It was an important organization for increasing voter participation among black citizens in the state.

I had some involvement in Andy Young's campaign, although my main job was nonpartisan. Later, I arranged for Andy Young to meet Hamilton Jordan and Governor Carter to help build relationships between the governor's staff and black community leaders. Andy Young was a key figure in bridging those connections during the early 1970s in Georgia.
cues: meeting with Hamilton Jordan and Governor Carter

summary: Fred Bennett was a member of Reverend Martin Luther King Sr.'s church, Ebenezer Baptist Church, and was involved in Governor Carter's campaign. He asked me to help organize a black statewide list of invitees for the governor's inaugural ball, which led to my eventual employment in the governor's office. Fred Bennett played a role in connecting me to the political process in Georgia.

Reverend Martin Luther King Sr. was the pastor of Ebenezer Baptist Church, where Fred Bennett was a member. Through connections with this church community, I became involved in organizing for Governor Carter's inaugural ball. The church was a significant institution in Atlanta's black community during the early 1970s.

Ebenezer Baptist Church in Atlanta was where Reverend Martin Luther King Sr. was pastor and where Fred Bennett was a member. This church was central to the black community and political organizing, including involvement in Governor Carter's campaign. My connection to Fred Bennett and the church helped me get involved in the governor's inaugural ball planning.

Hamilton Jordan was Governor Carter's executive secretary when I joined the governor's office. I worked closely with him to orient the governor's staff about key black community leaders and helped arrange meetings with people like Andrew Young. Hamilton was instrumental in bringing me into the governor's staff and supporting efforts to improve race relations in Georgia.

A. B. Padgett was the chairman of the Governor's Council on Human Relations and director of Community Affairs for the Trust Company of Georgia. He led the council during its early years after its creation by Governor Carter in 1971. Padgett's leadership helped establish the council's biracial structure and its mission to mediate racial issues in Georgia.
cues: chairman of Governor's Council on Human Relations

summary: The Trust Company of Georgia was where A. B. Padgett worked as director of Community Affairs. Padgett also chaired the Governor's Council on Human Relations, linking the company indirectly to the council's efforts. The Trust Company played a role in supporting community and race relations initiatives in Georgia during the early 1970s.

The Office of Economic Opportunity provided a $20,000 grant that helped fund the Governor's Council on Human Relations for its first year. My salary was transferred to this grant when the council was created. The OEO's support was crucial in establishing the council's operations in Georgia during the early 1970s.
cues: funding Governor's Council on Human Relations

summary: Senator Warren G. Magnuson introduced a bill to give the Governor's Council on Human Relations commission status, but the bill was killed in committee. This effort showed some legislative interest in strengthening the council's authority, though it ultimately did not pass during that session. Magnuson's involvement was part of the broader political context around race relations in the early 1970s.

Georgia was the state where I worked extensively on race relations and community organizing in the early 1970s. I was involved with the Governor's Council on Human Relations, voter registration drives, and connecting black leaders to the governor's office. The state had about forty-nine biracial councils, and Governor Carter was committed to improving black participation in state government during this period.

Alabama was mentioned as one of the southern states that, along with Mississippi and Georgia, did not have biracial councils at the time of our survey in the early 1970s. This context helped motivate Governor Carter to establish Georgia's Governor's Council on Human Relations. Alabama's situation was part of the broader regional challenges in race relations during that era.

Mississippi, like Alabama and Georgia, was one of the southern states without biracial councils during the early 1970s. This fact was part of a survey we conducted that influenced the creation of Georgia's Governor's Council on Human Relations. Mississippi's racial climate was a significant factor in regional civil rights efforts at the time.

The Civil Service Commission in Georgia was involved in reviewing proposals to improve minority and female participation in state jobs. Our council recommended increasing the number of candidates considered from five to eight to help minorities gain employment, and the governor endorsed this proposal. The commission also worked with the Employee Relations Division to assist departments in affirmative action planning.

The Equal Employment Opportunity Commission office in Atlanta worked closely with Georgia's Employee Relations Division. They reviewed affirmative action plans submitted by state departments to ensure compliance and progress. Although I was somewhat skeptical about the effectiveness of affirmative action plans, the EEOC played an important role in promoting employment equity in Georgia state government.
